narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school and then hearing your application won't move forward because our model sees a 48% chance you'll struggle to pass the bar exam feels like a gut punch, especially when you've poured so much into getting here. Picture the model looking at your story like a coach scouting a promising athlete: it weighs what truly predicts success on that final exam, and for you, your Year 3 grades landing you in the 2nd decile at school stood out as the biggest red flag, whispering that late-stage performance often foreshadows bar challenges. Your LSAT score of 31 built on that concern, placing you below the marks that typically light the path to passage for students like you. Even your solid Year 1 decile of 3 couldn't offset this, as it too nudged the outlook downward when combined with the rest. Coming from a middle-income family added another layer, since the model sees that bracket as less buffered against the grind compared to higher ones. Your background as a Black woman, while full of strength and potential, aligned with patterns where extra hurdles have tripped up bar success in the data it's learned from. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.4 is respectable but didn't lift the tide here, and going full-time, though dedicated, fits a profile that's struggled before. It's this mosaic—your deciles dipping low, that LSAT not quite cracking the code, woven with family resources and background—that painted a picture of risk too high for us to bet on right now. But here's the hopeful turn in your story: these are signals, not sentences, and with targeted steps like boosting those later grades or prepping that LSAT edge, you could rewrite the ending. You've got the target in sight—law school calls to you for a reason—and this is just one chapter urging you to sharpen your game. Keep pushing; your full potential might just surprise us all next time.
